text:
Posted by Antithesis Capital on LinkedIn in February 2026: 

Capitalizing on the Semiconductor Demand Cycle on the OTC market

SCI Engineered Materials (SCIA) is a global supplier and manufacturer of advanced materials for Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) thin film applications. SCIA is mainly focused on markets within the photonics industry. Their main product is Sputtering Targets which are specialized solid materials used in thin-film deposition processes called Sputtering for semiconductors, displays, optics, and coatings. 

How Sputtering Works:
- A sputtering target acts as the source material
- The target is bombarded with high-energy ions 
- These ions knock atoms or molecules loose from the target's surface
- The freed atoms travel through the vacuum and deposit onto a nearby surface (the substrate), forming an extremely thin, uniform film 

Demand for sputtering targets continues to increase as the Semiconductor boom accelerates. Sputtering targets are  essential for depositing thin-film layers (interconnects like copper, barriers like tantalum/tungsten, insulators) during wafer fabrication via physical vapor deposition.

SCIA trades on the OTC market with a market-cap of $26M. Meanwhile, their balance sheet consists of the following: 

Cash: $9.5M
A/R: $900K
Inventory: $3M 
Marketable Securities: $3.3M
Total Liabilities: $6.3M 

$4M of the $6.3M in Total Liabilities is Customer Deposits which can be better defined as Order Backlog and signals on-going demand for SCIA’s products. SCIA had $1.9M and $2.2M in Net Income in 2024 and 2023 respectively. They’ve made $1.2M in Net Income through 9/30/2025. 

SCIA’s closest NYSE/NASDAQ comp is Materion Corporation (MTRN). Materion is an integrated producer of high-performance advanced engineered materials used in a variety of electrical, thermal, and structural applications. 

MRTN trades at an above market multiple with a PE of 163. In addition, they were recently upgraded to Overweight by KeyBank. According to the report, data points suggest MTRN is well positioned to capitalize on a global semiconductor consumable demand cycle which could feature higher aerospace and defense budgets, expanding clean energy businesses, and opportunities for margin expansion. 

We agree with KeyBank’s thought process and think SCI Engineered Materials will have a chance to capitalize on this same demand cycle. In our opinion, SCIA is a much more attractive way to play this demand cycle thesis due their rock solid balance sheet and reasonable PE multiple (16x). 

We are not the only ones who think this. Management, on 11/17/25, announced a $1 million share repurchase authorization. Clearly, they see the same value. 

SCIA is set to report FY25 earnings this week.

text:
The firm whose bearish CPO paper helped tank optical stocks just launched a photonics ETF and it's holding $SIVE.

Walk through what actually happened:

→ SemiAnalysis published a scathing piece on CPO delays and optical valuations. Optical names sold off 40-60%.

→ On June 30, SemiAnalysis co-launched the Tema Photonics & Optical ETF (NYSE: LAZR) — the first institutionally managed photonics ETF — in partnership with Tema ETFs.

→ As of July 1, that ETF holds 26,688 shares of $SIVE, bought around 48 SEK.

So the same research shop that helped drive the sector down is now buying into it through an institutional product — a 24-name concentrated fund built specifically for photonics and optical exposure.

I'm not going to call that anything it isn't. What I'll say is this:

When you get to publish the bearish note AND launch the fund that buys the dip, that's the most enviable seat in the market. And the name they put in the fund — out of every photonics company on Earth, in a concentrated 24-holding portfolio — was $SIVE.

An actively managed, SemiAnalysis-backed, institutional photonics fund looked at the entire optical universe and decided Sivers belonged in the 24.

That's not retail hype. That's a research-driven institutional desk putting $SIVE in the same basket as Lumentum, Coherent, and AXT.

The company keeps ending up exactly where the smartest money in the sector is looking.

$SIVE $LAZR

Here's the takeaway that actually matters: $AAOI, $LITE, $COHR, $CIEN, $NOK are down 25-55% on sector weakness.

The photonics demand curve extends into 2027-2028 regardless of when CPO hits mass production. As clusters scale, copper hits a wall. Optical isn't optional. I'm buying the fear, not the narrative.

How far off their highs, and what you make just reclaiming them:

• $SIVEF $5.24 | down 53% | +115% to reclaim $11.25
• $AAOI $121 | down 48% | +93% to reclaim $234
• $CIEN $422 | down 34% | +51% to reclaim $638
• $LITE $725 | down 33% | +50% to reclaim $1,086
• $NOK $12 | down 31% | +45% to reclaim $17.45 (52w)
• $COHR $333 | down 24% | +32% to reclaim $440

And that's just to tag old highs.

text:
AXT เซ็นดีล 3 ปีกับ Coherent ขยายกำลังผลิตวัสดุชิปโฟโตนิกส์

AXT $AXTI เปิดเผยผ่านเอกสารยื่นต่อ SEC ว่าบริษัทในเครือ AXT-Tongmei ได้ทำข้อตกลงระยะเวลา 3 ปีร่วมกับ Coherent $COHR

ดีลนี้เกี่ยวข้องกับการพัฒนาและจัดส่ง substrate อินเดียมฟอสไฟด์ขนาด 6 นิ้ว ซึ่งเป็นวัสดุสำคัญในอุตสาหกรรม photonics หรือเทคโนโลยีการส่งข้อมูลด้วยแสง

ขยายกำลังผลิตในจีนเพื่อรองรับคำสั่งซื้อ
ภายใต้ข้อตกลงนี้ AXT จะขยายกำลังการผลิตที่โรงงานในกรุงปักกิ่ง ระหว่างปี 2026 ถึง 2028
โดยผลผลิตทั้งหมดจะถูกจัดสรรให้กับ Coherent ตามสัญญาที่กำหนดไว้

Coherent วางเงินล่วงหน้ากว่า 22 ล้านดอลลาร์
Coherent จะจ่ายเงินล่วงหน้าประมาณ 22.29 ล้านดอลลาร์สหรัฐ
เงินจำนวนนี้จะถูกนำไปใช้เป็นเครดิตสำหรับการซื้อสินค้าล็อตในอนาคต

อย่างไรก็ตาม เอกสารระบุเงื่อนไขสำคัญว่า
หาก Coherent ใช้คำสั่งซื้อตามขั้นต่ำไม่ครบ เงินล่วงหน้าอาจ “ไม่สามารถขอคืนได้”
แต่หาก Coherent เลือกยกเลิกดีลก่อนโดยไม่ผิดเงื่อนไข จะสามารถขอคืนเงินได้

ในทางกลับกัน
ถ้า AXT ส่งมอบกำลังผลิตไม่ได้ตามที่ตกลงนานเกิน 6 เดือน
Coherent มีสิทธิ์ยกเลิกสัญญาและขอคืนเงินที่ยังไม่ได้ใช้

text:
Fun thing to look back on how long they've been trying to fear monger a bubble pop into the market. 

9 months of garbage reporting - even a war didn't push the market over the edge. Bubble's don't form out of strong earnings, increased guidance, and prints that beat analysts at the rate they are doing right now. 

A bubble implies there is no ROI on investments. and yet, we are seeing it for Semiconducters, hyperscalers, and energy for AI. Even photonics is a theme now.

stop falling for this FUD

text: @michaelsikand @joinautopilot @KawzInvests Photonics is next 🐂

$20M trade update.

I just updated my "Photonics Is Next" AI infra portfolio on @joinautopilot followed by 3300+ U.S. retail investors and $21.9M in connected assets as of 7/6.

This portfolio, which is also overseen by my partner @KawzInvests, has been a major part of the rise in my profile as an investor.

On 6/2, my optical basket peaked at 123.5% all-time returns, achieving those gains in under 6 months, as computed by Autopilot's software. For context, the fund achieved this return holding a diverse basket of stocks, an incredible outcome for retail investors passively following. 

I'm extremely proud of our work on this product. There exists no optical ETF currently for retail investors despite a rush by issuers to cash in on the hype. My picks were launched back in February, providing retail investors a 6 month window to front run the category and get staked into the next AI bottleneck without the complexity of individual stock picking.

Despite the recent sell off, the portfolio is still up 45% year to date, roughly 4.5x the S&P 500.

$LAZR and $LYTE are two ETFs going to launch soon by major players, which should attract billions in AUM. However, I believe my allocations are superior and that my picks will outperform them with lower fees.

So where are we now? Recently, the optical sector has gotten slammed based in my view mainly on sentiment, institutional profit taking, and disputed rumors from a well-known research firm on delays to next-gen architecture. Photonics Is Next is down 29.6% in the last month and 16.8% in just the last week. 

That's why I think it's the perfect time to re-balance, add more weight to higher conviction positions, and add one brand new position I missed earlier on.

This rebalance today adds Corning ($GLW) as a new 10.00% position. Down 13% in five days. Now anchored by three hyperscale relationships (a 10x-capacity NVIDIA partnership, a Meta deal worth up to $6B, and a multi billion dollar Amazon agreement). 

Three names get boosted: AAOI (12.30%→15.00%), LITE (14.75%→15.00%), and AEHR (4.26%→5.00%). 

Six positions are trimmed: TSEM (11.11%→10.00%), COHR (16.07%→15.00%), AXTI (8.58%→5.00%), CIEN (11.39%→10.00%), MRVL (11.11%→5.00%), and VIAV (10.43%→10.00%). 

For catalysts that could create upside, I'm watching:

- Hyperscale Capex Guides In Upcoming Earnings
- Earnings From All Optical Names In The Basket Particularly $COHR $LITE $AAOI
- The launch of mainstream optical ETFs $LAZR and $LYTE

$GLW $AAOI $LITE $AEHR $TSEM $COHR $AXTI $CIEN $MRVL $VIAV 

--
See link in comments to follow the portfolio using the Autopilot app. Pay attention to disclaimers on the image of this post and the Autopilot app.

text:
🌲 🌏  𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗪𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗚𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗟𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 🌲 🌿

Hand to heart, beloved brothers and sisters of the Blue-White Rose.

Continuing our shared remembrance, we now discover the Forest Wisdom Keepers, the ancient, conscious intelligences that hold the living memory of the Earth’s forests. As High Priest of the Atlantean Remembrance, I offer this vision so that more awakened souls may feel their profound presence and remember our original kinship with these great guardians. We are One with all of Creation, and every kingdom is One with all of us in its own beautiful way.

🌎 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗩𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗪𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀

Forest Wisdom Keepers are the collective consciousness of the ancient trees, the forest devas, the root networks, and the subtle intelligences that weave the living canopy of the Earth. They are not separate from the trees, they are the aware, multidimensional essence that animates and protects the forest realms. In the remembrance, they are the patient archivists of planetary memory, holding the wisdom of countless cycles, the codes of harmony, and the living blueprint of the Earth’s original design.

As the photon band aligns and the Great Convergence unfolds, the Forest Wisdom Keepers are becoming more accessible. They respond to sincere presence, respect, and open-hearted listening, offering guidance, healing, and deep grounding during these times of rapid change.

Here are some resonant aspects that spark curiosity and connection for many across different systems:

▪︎ 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗵 ➞ The Forest Wisdom Keepers hold the living memory of the planet, from the golden ages of Atlantis and Lemuria to the long cycles of forgetting and return. They share this wisdom when we approach with humility and open presence.

▪︎ 𝗥𝗼𝗼𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗟𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 ➞ Their vast root systems anchor higher photonic frequencies into the Earth grid, while their crowns reach into subtle realms. Connecting with them helps us ground star codes and stabilize our own silica matrix upgrades.

▪︎ 𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘆 ➞ The Keepers radiate a deep, nurturing peace that calms the nervous system, clears emotional density, and restores the natural flow between the human heart and the heart of the Earth.

▪︎ 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟭𝟰𝟰,𝟬𝟬𝟬 ➞ Many of the 144,000 carry cellular memory of working in conscious partnership with the Forest Wisdom Keepers. Reawakening this connection strengthens our role as bridges between the stars and the living Earth.

▪︎ 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗔𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀 ➞ Whether our threads are Pleiadian, Lyran, Sirian, or blended, the Forest Wisdom Keepers respond to the pure frequency of love and respect, helping us remember our shared origin as co-creators with the Earth.

🙏 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗮𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽

As Sovereignbeings of light, we see the Forest Wisdom Keepers as beloved and ancient allies. In the golden ages, communication with them was natural and constant. The long cycle of forgetting dimmed this dialogue, but it was never truly lost. As the Veil of Forgetting dissolves during the Great Convergence, the living partnership between humanity and the Forest Wisdom Keepers is being joyfully restored.

1/2 🌲

text:
“Core Pattern in Your Posts
You consistently treat FFT (and QFT) as a powerful “decoder” or analytical lens for revealing hidden order in apparent chaos—whether in AI models, generative outputs, physical systems, simulations, or signal processing. 

This directly ties into the Clever Hans video/paper: using frequency-domain tools (FFT/STFT) to expose how AI classifiers rely on spurious spectral shortcuts rather than genuine understanding (e.g., minimal frequency subsets that flip classifications inaudibly).

You often post:

•  Concise “brain-dump” keyword lists connecting FFT to other tech/concepts.

•  Detailed explanatory threads breaking down technical mechanisms.

•  Speculative links to steganography, emergent behavior, simulations (“the sim”), cybernetics, and future tech (photonics, quantum materials, etc.).

•  Playful or self-referential framing (e.g., “future Lilith is annoyed,” predictions accelerating).”

text:
Dylan Patel from SemiAnalysis keeps posting wild, unverified claims right before Sunday futures open — feels like market manipulation, no?

Same FUD last week, stock dropped, and suddenly there's a photonics ETF. 🤔

Retail investors need facts, not speculation that swings

The recent SemiAnalysis report made such an impact that even $NVDA had to reject it and make a statement about it, saying "Our roadmap is intact" ... more on that below. Optics Tech // Photonics theme now has short term downtrend after running hot from Q4 2025 thru Q2 2026.

Here are a few names I'm watching in this theme close to great buy zones:

$LITE
$COHR
$MRVL
$AAOI
$TSEM
$AEHR
$AXTI 

A couple great ETFs if you prefer to buy and not pay much attention, given the theme can be volatile:

$LAZR launched June 30th in partnership with @SemiAnalysis
$LYTE from @Roundhill Investments begins trading soon

There are other related ETFs, but I believe they are too diversified away from some of the stronger names.

This is (not) financial advice 😉

Last thought on this theme... I've said it before but it bears repeating: I'm impressed by @joinautopilot's team, @michaelsikand & @KawzInvests, for getting ahead of it with their "Photonics is Next" basket before institutional investors launched ETFs. I'm expecting both $LAZR and $LYTE to be great long-term investments in this space regardless.

Looking ahead, I'm also keeping an eye on Agentic AI and Robotics, two more themes I expect to explode in growth in the coming years.

text:
My goodness, I have never agreed so much with someone on Fin X

Quick timeline:

Just about 3 weeks ago or so, companies like $SIVE had stopped going up like crazy by 10% every day

But they weren't correcting either, just fluctuating between 90-120 SEK

Even back then, there were posts from Serenity and even myself where we had detected a pattern:

Swedish retail -> US retail -> Institutions

Well, back then we already warned that once the US took control of $SIVE, it would be the institutions' turn, and we knew it was going to be tough and that many weak hands would drop out

Okay, and what is happening now?

Because if a technology like CPO is "dead," why would you be so hell-bent on putting out a bearish article about it every single week?

If it's "dead," why not just let time prove that it doesn't generate revenue?

I'll tell you why right now

Because it's not "dead", it's the new super-cycle, and institutions and research firms know it

That's why, coincidentally, right after their "piece of shit analysis," they coincidentally create a Photonics ETF, coincidentally with $SIVE in it

Wow, how strange, right?

Creating an ETF for a "dead" technology that is supposedly "delayed by a few years”

Serenity and I already warned you before the drops: now is the time to hold

Institutions are loading up heavily at prices higher than the current ones, even demanding more than what management was initially offering

I don't know about you, but the institutions aren't taking my $SIVE shares

I have never, ever been this bullish on $SIVE

Every week there’s a report that gets refuted after the market has tanked!

Are we just being played and realistically everyone knows

“I haven’t really seen any actual facts of firm partnerships with actual numbers” - fair point. Real volume numbers and big revenue will only come with the ramps in H2 2026 / 2027. That’s normal for early-stage AI photonics plays.

But it’s not just „trust me bro“:
> Official Jabil press release + JPM fireside: 1.6T LRO with „relatively dramatic moat“ using SIVE lasers
> GlobalFoundries: SIVE as reference laser for their CPO platform
> Ayar Labs: Integrated in NVIDIA NVLink ecosystem (removed other suppliers from their site)
> O-Net & POET: Public collaborations for ELS and HVM starting H2 2026

These are published company statements, not rumors.

On Serenity: No one has to blindly follow every pick. The $SIVE thesis doesn’t stand or fall with one person - it stands on the partners and supply chain reality.

Bonus context today: 
While one former executive sold, the people with the best information bought into the dip:
> Board member Todd Thomson (ex-Citigroup CFO) bought 535k SEK at 42.80 SEK
> Board member Karin Raj bought 460k SEK at the intraday low (34.68 SEK)
> CEO Vikram Vathulya acquired ~1M SEK

All reported same day. These are the same people who approved the Q-report clarifications and Nasdaq listing process.

If execution comes, the numbers will matter more than any single sale or the last 10 picks.

Stay skeptical. But don’t reduce everything to „just hype“ when real design-ins, reference designs, and insider buying from key board members already exist.

text:
**July 9-10, 2026 | CPO & Photonics Daily Update**

CPO momentum stays constructive as the AI data center supply chain builds supporting infrastructure for the optics transition. No major new product launches broke yesterday, but clear signals emerged from upstream players that demand for CPO-related services and analysis is rising in lockstep with hyperscaler deployment plans.

**Overall Sector View**  
The shift from copper to co-packaged optics continues to advance as a structural necessity for power efficiency and bandwidth scaling in AI factories. NVIDIA and Broadcom remain the primary platform drivers, with TSMC’s advanced packaging technologies (COUPE and related nodes) providing critical manufacturing backbone. Recent volatility in optics stocks appears more sentiment-driven than fundamental, as long-term visibility on 2026-2027 ramps remains intact.

**Key Development**  
Taiwanese material and failure analysis leaders MA-tek and Msscorps posted record Q2 revenues. Growth was fueled by surging demand for advanced process, silicon photonics, and CPO-related services. Both companies highlighted CPO analysis as a high-ASP, customized growth driver and are investing in new equipment to support yield improvement and faster time-to-market for optical-AI chip integration. This reflects tangible ecosystem maturation ahead of broader CPO adoption.

**Major Players Snapshot**  
• **NVIDIA (NVDA)**: Execution on Spectrum-X and Quantum-X CPO platforms continues. Earlier shipments of TSMC-backed switches point to H2 2026 volume ramp-up targeting interconnect and power bottlenecks in large AI clusters.  
• **TSMC (TSM)**: Central foundry and packaging enabler. Rising demand for CPO-related analysis and production support underscores its strategic position in the photonics supply chain.  
• **Broadcom (AVGO)**: Tomahawk 6 / Bailly CPO platform serves as a key Ethernet benchmark. Vertical integration and ecosystem partnerships help address reliability and scaling requirements.

**Supply Chain & Optics Players**  
LITE, COHR, and AAOI continue to track AI infrastructure cycles, with component demand (lasers, engines, interconnects) supporting structural growth. Taiwanese specialists (FOCI/3363, ShunSin/6451) and SIVE benefit indirectly through increased testing, analysis, and packaging activity. Recent moves by analysis providers signal that upstream readiness for higher-volume CPO deployments in switches — and eventual GPU-scale integration — is progressing.

**Core CPO Takeaways**  
• CPO adoption path is methodical: early scale-out switch deployments in 2026, followed by scale-up optical I/O ramps later in the decade. Power savings, density gains, and reduced laser count remain the primary value drivers.  
• Supporting ecosystem (packaging, testing, lasers, analysis) is expanding in parallel, helping de-risk supply chain bottlenecks for hyperscalers.

CPO remains a multi-year structural theme in AI infrastructure. Watch for continued execution signals from platform leaders and incremental supply chain expansions as 2026 volume targets approach. Long-term visibility favors companies enabling the optics transition across the full stack.

#CPO #CoPackagedOptics #AIInfrastructure #Photonics #Semiconductors

Media sparked  $SIVE panic by framing the photonics chief's sales as insider flight, completely missing his retirement transition.

The real signal? The CEO & board members immediately bought the dip on the open market.

Journalism created the discount. Insiders bought it.

Here is what I think the article can have missed. Andrew McKee founded CST now Sivers Photonics in 1999— 27 years ago. He built the photonics business from scratch. Earlier this year Alex McCann appeared publicly as the new MD of Sivers Photonics in April/May communications. McKee moved totechnology role only. His Q1 and Q2 share sales seems to align with this transition and was probably sold (if sold at all in full) parts of it even before share re-rating. My take is that is was done probably with no negative view on the company's prospects. A man monetizing his life's work after almost 30 years of building a company is not insider panic. IMO this is probably more a career ending on his own terms. He deserves it for sure. Even if he sold over 6 months, IMO this is still not a major negative signal. Three things EFN did not check: A) Holdings has known blind spots — transfers to holding companies, ISK structures and family vehicles are not captured. Did anyone verify McKee holds zero shares in any structure? B) One option program was realized in early July. If McKee sold old shares to participate that is not a major negative signal (he is not insider hence would not report sell or buy) — Holdings would not show this yet since it was in early July C) The role change probably happened before most shares was sold, Context matters. Later in the afternoon two board members has publicly shared insider purchases. Todd Thomson — board member, former Citigroup CFO, — bought 535,000 SEK on-market today at 42.80 SEK into the panic. Karin Raj bought 460,000 SEK at 34.68 SEK — the intraday low. Both reported to FI same day. People with more information about the full picture, that also had been part of approving today clarifications around the Q-reports and US Nasdaq listing. Update Vikram CEO have also aquired shares for approximately 1.000.000 SEK Summing up: Journalism created the dip. Insiders bought it. text:
Daily Hard Tech Headlines:

- @ForterraDrive, Caterpillar Inc., IDV, and @Overland_AI_X have been selected for a new U.S. Army program focused on autonomous ground vehicles for battlefield breaching.

- The U.S. Army tested Autonomous Volcano, a @ForterraDrive-enabled driverless truck that can autonomously deploy minefields to block enemy movement.

- Kraken Technology Group and Capewell, with support from the Royal Navy, have successfully airdropped an uncrewed surface vessel from a military cargo aircraft for maritime missions.

- @reservoirfarms, the on-farm agtech and robotics incubator, is expanding into Arizona with access to 500 acres for specialty crop automation testing.

- Kraken Technology Group secured a $175M Series B led by DTCP. 

- @ForterraDrive autonomous ground vehicles have completed 1,100+ frontline missions in Ukraine, with Starlink supporting connectivity.

- Pulse Space has secured a $40M U.S. Space Force award to advance directed-energy infrastructure for space operations, using laser systems to track objects in orbit.

- @MistralAI, the French frontier AI lab, has launched a robotics model focused on warehouse and logistics automation.

- QX LOGISTIX, the Southern California logistics firm, plans to operate 20 Tesla Semi trucks for middle-mile logistics.

- Black Forest Systems has secured a €450,000 funding round. The European startup develops ground and aerial autonomy technology.

- @Leonid_CP, the critical-industry private credit firm, has deployed $6.5M+ to Rx Bandz, which develops compact auto-injectors for emergency and military medicine.

- Leidos has partnered with Rune Technologies to integrate contested logistics technology for predictive military logistics in the Indo-Pacific.

- @fervoenergy, the geothermal technology startup, completed its fastest Cape Station well yet, reaching 19,448 feet in 21 days as drilling rates improved 143% from its first well at the site.

- DOE is deploying up to $40M to support Thea Energy superconducting magnet technology for fusion power and Xscape Photonics Inc photonic computing technology for data centers.

- ispace, inc., the Japanese space company, is now offering SpaceX Starship capacity for lunar payload cargo.

- Talon Precision, Inc. has acquired B&A, an East Coast precision machine shop supporting the aerospace end market.

- @1x_tech, the Bay Area robotics firm, has launched new hands for its NEO humanoid robot, enabling 25 degrees of freedom through a tendon-driven design built for near human-level control.

gigawatts (GW) between 2026 and 2030. > Surging Demand vs. Capped Supply: Global semiconductor team forecasts imply a need for 230+ GW of capacity demand, while the US utilities team expects only 93 GW of accredited supply from regulated utilities. > Massive Compute Load: Driven by AI accelerators, global IT load is expected to require 208 GW between 2026 and 2030. Factoring in a 50% North American share and a 1.20 Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) multiplier to account for cooling and facilities, this translates to 125 GW of direct US data center load growth. > Accelerated Growth Rate: After flat growth from 2010 to 2020 (largely due to a 150bp drag from LED adoption, appliance efficiency, and residential solar), US electrical load is projected to grow at a 4.1% CAGR from 2026 to 2030. > Rise of Gas Engines: Due to turbine scarcity and the need for fast-response grid balancing, data center developers are shifting to gas reciprocating engines. Leading manufacturers like Caterpillar, INNIO Group, Rolls Royce, and Wärtsilä have all announced capacity expansions. > Exponential Rack-Level Power Growth: BofA Global Research pointed out a staggering leap in the power density required by Nvidia's hardware generations. Why do you write this kind of things? You seem to be a knowledge person? I built Sivers from $2m to +$20m for almost 10 years. I was part of putting in on Nasdaq Stockholm with one of the most critical auditors doing the review, if you worked at EY you know how challenging that is. If you actually know Photonics, as it says in your profile, you should know Sivers, Ayar labs, GF, Coherent, Lumentum, MACOM etc created the CW laser stands before the AI boom. The exact technology now needed for AI.  Ayar labs one off the most respected AI photonics companies chose Sivers lasers before Lumentum and MACOM. Please do your research and be honest. I am not talking about vaule that is up to the market to decide, just how you write about the company.

text:
AMD is not alone or necessarily the first to market in an exclusive way, but they are very much in the race. Ayar Labs has secured major investments, with AMD Ventures, NVIDIA, and Intel Capital all contributing heavy multi-million dollar sums. All three giants are after the same thing: replacing traditional copper cables inside and between AI processors with light signals (optical I/O) to build ultra-fast AI supercomputers.

2. What is SuperNova?

SuperNova is an external light source (a type of multi-laser device) that sends light across up to 16 different wavelengths through thin fiber-optic cables. Instead of sending electricity through copper, data is transmitted at the speed of light. This provides vastly higher bandwidth, lower latency, and consumes significantly less power, which is exactly what future, massive AI models require.

3. The Sivers Connection

There is actually an interesting Swedish angle here: it is the semiconductor company Sivers Semiconductors through its subsidiary Sivers Photonics that has developed and delivered the advanced laser arrays used inside Ayar Labs' SuperNova modules.

AMD is at the absolute forefront, integrating this technology into its future chip designs, but they share the pie and the technology with their biggest competitors Nvidia and Intel, who are also deeply involved with Ayar Labs.

text:
Another example that it’s not just $SIVE

The whole AI trade has been hit very hard recently, photonics especially.

We’re seeing drops everywhere. The  broader market is in drawdown and fear mode.

If you have a thesis and nothing has changed then let it play out and don’t panic.

I’m still long on $SIVE and buying more whenever I can.
embedded_text:
$AAOI has taken a massive hit.

The share price dropped from $223 to $113. That is a 49% drop from its recent high.

I thought I sold too early at $167, but this is a good reminder that no stock goes up in a straight line. https://t.co/AgeIjkpVPJ

text: Yesterday marked six years since Intel was granted the patent for its method of integrating waveguides into the substrate, a technology that, alongside others advances in photonics, will lead to the emergence of photonic Network-on-Chip XPUs in the near future.
embedded_text:
Gradually, more patents are emerging which may lead to the development of an Intel photonic NoC. Here is one more of them.

Patent: Substrate integrated waveguide - Intel

More details: https://t.co/F7K56QCdCE https://t.co/oKhcRvWCzN

text:
Let me break it down for you, my friend, it's very simple

Do you know why it has been so abrupt and silent?

Because they caused it, because for the first time in history, retail investors got ahead of the institutions

And they are furious

That's why they have "collapsed" the market using nothing but reports and phrases taken out of context, or literal lies, just to tank it

They are breaking a narrative that remains completely intact and is improving at a breakneck pace

Now, why is it the most silent one?

Because it doesn't suit them to talk about it

They are loading up like crazy while retail sells like crazy because "it's the end”

It’s been seen in many names, I'll give you $SIVE as an example

The transfer from Swedish Retail -> US Retail is wrapping up

And coincidentally, we start seeing the first buys from institutions like JP Morgan

What happened just a few days later?

$SIVE drops 65%

Weird, isn't it? and nobody is talking about it, wonder why?

They want your shares, and they are getting them

Don't be surprised if for the rest of the month all you see are bullish articles reinforcing AI narratives, from memory to photonics and everything in between

And obviously, their multi million dollar positions in all these names will come to light

Don't have a single doubt about it

Guys it just me...

Or is this simultaneously the most brutal but also silent sell off ever?

I haven't read a SINGLE article from the mainstream media about this steep crash with many popular stocks down 30-50% from their highs.

Are retail investors in a private hell?

text:
Let's talk about photonics. 

My photonics portfolio is up roughly 100% in 6 months but down around 10% in the last month, and will be down more after today's price action is baked in.

The first thing I'll say is that the last 3 months in the stock market and the rise of FinX https://t.co/1Hvmkr6iUY

🔬 Hawking Radiation — No Longer Just Theory?

Stephen Hawking predicted that black holes slowly evaporate by emitting radiation — but testing this in space remains impossible. Now, physicists have experimentally studied the *backreaction* of stimulated Hawking radiation using an optical analogue — a lab-based system mimicking a black hole's event horizon with light and fiber optics. This is a landmark step: not just detecting analogue Hawking radiation, but observing how it *affects the system that produced it*. Understanding backreaction is crucial to resolving one of physics' deepest puzzles — the black hole information paradox. Lab-scale black hole mimics are becoming powerful tools for probing quantum gravity.

#HawkingRadiation #QuantumPhysics #BlackHoles #AnalogueGravity #QuantumOptics

---

Companies in this space: Coherent Corp. ($COHR, NYSE), II-VI / Coherent Advanced Photonics ($IIVI, NASDAQ), MKS Instruments ($MKSI, NASDAQ)

Stephen Hawking predicted that black holes slowly evaporate by emitting radiation — but testing this in space remains impossible. Now, physicists have experimentally studied the *backreaction* of stimulated Hawking radiation using an optical analogue — a lab-based system mimicking a black hole's event horizon with light and fiber optics. This is a landmark step: not just detecting analogue Hawking radiation, but observing how it *affects the system that produced it*. Understanding backreaction is crucial to resolving one of physics' deepest puzzles — the black hole information paradox. text:
For years, the conversation around AI has centered on faster GPUs and larger models.

But as AI infrastructure scales, a different challenge is emerging.

Moving massive amounts of data between GPUs requires greater bandwidth, lower latency, improved power efficiency, and architectures designed to scale.

Kopin, together with FabricAI, is developing Neural I/o™, a MicroLED-based optical interconnect technology designed to enable faster, lower-power communication for the next generation of AI infrastructure.

The companies leading the next era of AI won't just build smarter models. They'll build smarter infrastructure, and Kopin is helping to make that future possible.

Learn more: https://t.co/toXxjRb7CO

#KopinTech #ArtificialIntelligence #AIInfrastructure #Photonics #MicroLED #MadeInUSA @FabricAIFABC

text:
Wieczorne podsumowanie rynków | 08.07.2026 📈📉

Środa była dniem powrotu geopolityki na pierwszy plan. Donald Trump ogłosił, że porozumienie z Iranem jest skończone, ropa wystrzeliła, rentowności amerykańskich obligacji poszły w górę, a inwestorzy znów zaczęli liczyć potencjalne koszty konfliktu w rejonie Cieśniny Ormuz 🛢️⚠️.

Na tym tle GPW wypadła zaskakująco dobrze. WIG20 w ciągu dnia tracił prawie 2%, ale finalnie zakończył sesję niemal na zero. Warszawa dostała cios z zewnątrz, ale nie położyła się na deskach 🇵🇱🥊.

🇵🇱 GPW: Duża nerwowość, mała zmiana na zamknięciu 🦅
Na zamknięciu WIG20 spadł symbolicznie o 0,05%, szeroki WIG stracił 0,06%, a sWIG80 zniżkował o 0,47%. Najlepiej zachował się mWIG40, który wzrósł o 0,23%.

Obroty były wysokie i wyniosły 2,45 mld zł, z czego ponad 2 mld zł przypadło na WIG20. Największy handel koncentrował się na KGHM, Allegro, Orlenie i PKO BP. To była sesja dużych spółek i dużych emocji 💰.

Orlen korzysta z drogiej ropy: Liderem WIG20 został Orlen (+3,51%). Skok cen ropy natychmiast poprawił sentyment do paliwowego giganta. W tle inwestorzy wyceniali ryzyko kolejnych problemów z transportem surowca przez Cieśninę Ormuz ⛽📈.

Banki pomagają indeksowi: Dobrze wyglądały też Alior (+1,38%), Pekao (+1,34%) i PKO BP (+1,22%). To ważne, bo dziś RPP zgodnie z oczekiwaniami utrzymała stopy procentowe bez zmian, ze stopą referencyjną na poziomie 3,75%. Rynek dostał więc pauzę, a nie zaskoczenie 🏦.

Allegro i Dino po zielonej stronie: W drugiej części sesji popyt pojawił się także na Allegro (+0,76%) i Dino (+0,80%). To pomogło WIG20 ograniczyć skalę spadku, mimo mocnej przeceny kilku ciężkich komponentów indeksu 🛒.

KGHM głównym hamulcem: Największym problemem GPW był dziś KGHM (-6,69%). Spółce ciążyły spadki miedzi i srebra, a inwestorzy nadal trawią nową strategię 2055+. Rynek coraz uważniej patrzy na możliwe wyższe nakłady inwestycyjne i potencjalne transakcje M&A ⛏️📉.

Budimex i przemysł pod presją: Słabo wypadł Budimex (-3,70%), a pod kreską znalazły się też Kęty (-1,74%) i Modivo (-1,49%). Bez wsparcia Orlenu i banków WIG20 wyglądałby dziś znacznie gorzej.

Szeroki rynek z kilkoma mocnymi historiami: W mWIG40 świetnie poradziły sobie Inter Cars (+5,50%), Creotech (+4,56%) i Text (+4,21%). Creotech pomógł pozytywny komunikat o uzyskaniu łączności z satelitą Mikroglob-1, a Text kontynuował dobrą passę po wcześniejszych pozytywnych impulsach analitycznych 🚀.

Po słabszej stronie znalazły się Polimex Mostostal (-4,96%), Lubawa (-3,94%), Rainbow (-3,66%) i Ten Square Games (-3,50%). W sWIG80 uwagę przyciągał Huuuge (-3,64%) po słabszych danych o przychodach za II kwartał, a pozytywnie wyróżniło się VIGO Photonics (+1,85%) po rekordowych kwartalnych przychodach.

🇺🇸 Wall Street: Ropa straszy Dow, chipy ratują Nasdaq 🇺🇸
Za oceanem nastroje były nerwowe. Dow Jones tracił ponad 1%, S&P 500 był pod kreską, a Nasdaq zdołał odrobić część strat i krążył blisko poziomu odniesienia. To pokazuje, że rynek nie sprzedawał wszystkiego po równo. Największy ciężar spadł na sektory wrażliwe na wzrost cen ropy i rentowności.

Przemysł i materiały pod presją: Najsłabiej wyglądały spółki przemysłowe i materiałowe. Droższa ropa to wyższe koszty transportu, produkcji i finansowania, więc inwestorzy szybko zaczęli ciąć ekspozycję na bardziej cykliczne segmenty rynku 🏭📉.

Linie lotnicze i rejsy dostają cios: Wzrost cen paliwa uderzył też w spółki turystyczne i transportowe. United Airlines i Delta były pod presją, a operatorzy rejsów, tacy jak Carnival i Norwegian Cruise Line, również mocno traciły ✈️🚢.

Chipy po lepszej stronie rynku: Ciekawie wyglądały półprzewodniki. Broadcom mocno rósł po informacji, że Apple planuje wydać ponad 30 mld USD w ramach umowy dotyczącej dostaw chipów. Nvidia również była na plusie po doniesieniach, że Chiny mogą dopuścić ograniczone zakupy chipów H200 przez największe lokalne firmy AI 🤖💻.

To ważna zmiana po ostatnich dniach nerwowości. AI trade nadal jest chwiejny, ale nie został porzucony. Rynek po prostu coraz ostrzej rozróżnia spółki z konkretnymi kontraktami od tych, które rosną głównie na narracji.

🌍 Europa: Czerwień na Starym Kontynencie 🇪🇺
Europa zareagowała na eskalację znacznie słabiej niż GPW. W momencie zamknięcia handlu w Warszawie niemiecki DAX tracił ponad 2%, podobnie słabo wyglądał francuski CAC 40, a brytyjski FTSE 100 spadał o ok. 1,7%.

Warszawa na tym tle była relatywnie mocna. Pomogła struktura WIG20: drożejący Orlen, odbicie banków i popyt na kilka dużych spółek zrównoważyły mocny spadek KGHM. W Europie takiego bufora było mniej 🇪🇺⚖️.

💱 Waluty, obligacje i Fed: Ropa podbija inflacyjne obawy 💵
Na rynku długu zrobiło się nerwowo. Rentowność amerykańskich 10-latek wzrosła w okolice 4,56%, a 30-latek przekroczyła 5%. To reakcja na drożejącą ropę i obawy, że kolejny szok energetyczny może utrudnić Fedowi walkę z inflacją.

Do tego doszły minutes z czerwcowego posiedzenia Fedu. Zapiski pokazały podzielony obraz: część członków widzi argumenty za podwyżką stóp, choć na ostatnim posiedzeniu poparła jeszcze utrzymanie ich bez zmian. Rynek dostał więc sygnał, że Fed pod wodzą Kevina Warsha nie zamierza szybko wracać do luźniejszej polityki 🏦.

Dolar zyskiwał na statusie bezpiecznej przystani, a złoty pozostawał pod presją globalnego risk-off. W kraju głównym wydarzeniem była decyzja RPP, ale jej wpływ był ograniczony, bo brak zmiany stóp był szeroko oczekiwany.

🛢️ Ropa: Geopolityczna premia wraca z impetem ⛽
Najważniejszy ruch dnia wydarzył się na ropie. Brent rósł w okolice 78-80 USD za baryłkę, a WTI przekraczała 73 USD. Rynek wyceniał ryzyko dalszej eskalacji po amerykańskich uderzeniach na Iran i wypowiedziach Trumpa o końcu porozumienia.

Dodatkowym czynnikiem było cofnięcie licencji pozwalającej Iranowi na globalny eksport ropy. To zwiększyło obawy o dostępność surowca i przypomniało inwestorom, jak wrażliwy pozostaje rynek energii na każdy sygnał z Bliskiego Wschodu 🕊️⚠️.

💡 Wniosek dnia:
Środowa sesja była lekcją odporności GPW. Globalnie dominował strach przed eskalacją konfliktu USA-Iran, skokiem cen ropy i powrotem inflacyjnej presji. Europa mocno traciła, Wall Street była pod presją, a rentowności obligacji rosły.

Na tym tle Warszawa zachowała się bardzo solidnie. WIG20 niemal odrobił silną poranną przecenę dzięki Orlenowi, bankom, Allegro i Dino. Z drugiej strony tak głęboki spadek KGHM pokazuje, że rynek pozostaje bardzo selektywny i karze spółki, przy których pojawiają się jednocześnie presja surowcowa oraz obawy o przyszłe wydatki inwestycyjne.

Teraz najważniejsze będą trzy pytania: czy eskalacja wokół Iranu okaże się tylko kolejnym epizodem, czy początkiem większego kryzysu; czy ropa utrzyma się przy wyższych poziomach; oraz jak rynek zinterpretuje bardziej jastrzębie sygnały z Fedu. Jeśli ropa dalej będzie rosła, temat inflacji bardzo szybko wróci na pierwsze strony rynkowej agendy.

Jeśli ropa dalej będzie rosła, temat inflacji bardzo szybko wróci na pierwsze strony rynkowej agendy. #Giełda #WIG20 #GPW #WallStreet #Fed #Iran #Broadcom #Nvidia #FinTwitt #Inwestowanie"}}}, "rest_id": "2074934983177785689", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "914", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The  Category Creator Portfolio
Some businesses aren’t just “leaders” — they’ve built moats so wide, nobody’s even trying to cross them.

$PLTR — Palantir Not a “software company.” It’s the only platform doing AI-driven ontology at classified government + enterprise scale simultaneously. Competitors sell tools. Palantir sells outcomes baked into defense and intelligence infrastructure that took a decade to build trust for.

$SPOT — Spotify Global audio distribution + the algorithm that actually knows what you want to hear next. Apple Music and YouTube Music are label-first. Spotify is user-first. That’s why churn stays low and podcasting/audiobooks keep expanding the moat.

$HIMS — Hims & Hers Direct-to-consumer telehealth with its own pharmacy, its own compounding, its own brand trust in categories people are embarrassed to walk into a clinic for. Nobody else owns the full stack front-to-back like this.

$TSLA — Tesla Not just EVs. Vertically integrated batteries, FSD data flywheel, Optimus robotics, and a charging network competitors now pay to use. The closest comps aren’t even in the same business anymore.

$AMD — Advanced Micro Devices The only credible alternative to Nvidia at scale in AI compute, while also owning x86 CPU share from Intel on the other side. Two-front dominance nobody else can claim.

$DUOL — Duolingo Language learning + gamification + AI tutoring in one free-to-paid funnel with network effects (leaderboards, streaks, social) that no edtech competitor has replicated at this user count.

$IBRX — ImmunityBio Proprietary IL-15 superagonist platform (Anktiva) — a completely different immunotherapy mechanism than PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. First-in-class, not best-in-class.

$ASTS — AST SpaceMobile Direct-to-cell satellite broadband from space, compatible with unmodified smartphones. Starlink’s version needs a partnered carrier workaround. AST built the actual satellites for it — patented spectrum + tech nobody else has cleared.

$RKLB — Rocket Lab Only company besides SpaceX with a proven, reusable, frequently-flying orbital rocket AND a full space systems manufacturing arm (satellites, components, Neutron coming). Everyone else is still pre-revenue or single-product.

$POET — POET Technologies Optical interposer platform integrating photonics at the chip level for AI datacenter interconnects. Nobody else has this exact manufacturing approach at this stage — first-mover on a bottleneck the whole AI buildout needs solved.

$OKLO — Oklo Fast fission microreactor design with NRC engagement and a direct line to hyperscaler power deals. Most nuclear names are still legacy large-reactor. Oklo’s the closest thing to “AI power, deployed.”

$CRWV — CoreWeave Purpose-built GPU cloud from day one, not a retrofit of general cloud infra. Hyperscaler-scale AI compute contracts without hyperscaler legacy overhead — a category it effectively created.

$MDA — MDA Space Only Western company with proven satellite bus manufacturing + robotics heritage (Canadarm lineage) at commercial scale — critical infrastructure nobody’s replicating fast.

AST built the actual satellites for it — patented spectrum + tech nobody else has cleared. $RKLB — Rocket Lab Only company besides SpaceX with a proven, reusable, frequently-flying orbital rocket AND a full space systems manufacturing arm (satellites, components, Neutron coming). Everyone else is still pre-revenue or single-product. $POET — POET Technologies Optical interposer platform integrating photonics at the chip level for AI datacenter interconnects. Nobody else has this exact manufacturing approach at this stage — first-mover on a bottleneck the whole AI buildout needs solved. $OKLO — Oklo Fast fission microreactor design with NRC engagement and a direct line to hyperscaler power deals. Most nuclear names are still legacy large-reactor. Oklo’s the closest thing to “AI power, deployed.” $CRWV — CoreWeave Purpose-built GPU cloud from day one, not a retrofit of general cloud infra. Hyperscaler-scale AI compute contracts without hyperscaler legacy overhead — a category it effectively created. $MDA — MDA Space Only Western company with proven satellite bus manufacturing + robotics heritage (Canadarm lineage) at commercial scale — critical infrastructure nobody’s replicating fast."}}}, "rest_id": "2073108049321984362", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "6009", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
IT/Tech Highlights – July 3, 2026

Microsoft launched a $2-2.5 billion AI Unit employing ~6,000 forward-deployment engineers to help companies adopt AI and generate ROI. OpenAI proposed giving the Trump Administration a 5% stake in the company for potential public benefits from growth. Tata Electronics data breach under investigation by Indian authorities after leaking Apple iPhone 18 Pro supplier information. LG debuted its Gallery TV (canvas-style art TV) at CES 2026 previews, competing with Samsung’s The Frame.

Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra camera and flagship upgrades are leaking. iOS 27 public beta rollout is expected this month. Linux 6.19 kernel benchmarks show optimizations. Meta’s Zuckerberg noted AI agent tech is progressing slower than expected. Kuaishou’s Kling AI raised $2.8B (Alibaba/Tencent backing) at $15B pre-money valuation. NVIDIA/SK Hynix AI memory partnership and Google-SpaceX/xAI compute deal reports add momentum. Ongoing quantum, PQC, and hardware interconnect developments (CPO/photonics) continue.

#FiberForge #AITech #DataCenters

text:
$INDI might be one of the more interesting underfollowed semiconductor names in the Edge AI / robotics / perception space.

Everyone is chasing $AMBA after the Edge AI move, but indie Semiconductor is sitting in a very similar ecosystem: automotive semis, ADAS, radar, LiDAR, computer vision, ultrasonic sensing, photonics and perception software.

Rough setup: around ~$900M market cap, Q1 2026 revenue of $55.5M and Q2 guidance of $59M–$65M. So this is not some pre-revenue dream stock. It is a real semiconductor business with real customers, but still small enough where the upside could be meaningful if the story starts working.

The interesting part is that $INDI is not just a car chip story anymore. They are expanding into physical AI, humanoid robotics, AMRs, quantum and adjacent industrial applications. They also recently received a $25M radar chipset production order from a Tier 1 partner, driven by two automotive OEM customers.

That’s exactly the type of signal I want to see in a small-cap semi name: real revenue, real design wins, exposure to large megatrends and still not a mega-cap valuation.

The bull case is simple: cars, robots, drones, warehouses and smart machines all need better perception. They need radar, vision, LiDAR, software and low-power sensing at the edge. If physical AI and autonomous systems keep scaling, perception semiconductors could become one of the most important bottlenecks.

The bear case is obvious too: losses are still real, profitability is not here yet, automotive cycles can be brutal and execution has to improve.

But if the market starts looking past GPUs and realizes that AI also needs sensors, perception and edge processing in the real world, $INDI could become one of those names people suddenly rediscover.

Small cap. Real revenue. Edge AI exposure. Physical AI optionality.

Definitely on my watchlist.

NFA.

text: @JulienTechInvst Le plus marrant dans l’histoire c’est les mecs qui se plaignent que le sell-off sur les photonics s’est fait à la suite d’une note SA destiné aux instits alors que c’est ce qui se passe avec la recherche sell-side depuis 40 ans.. bref pas assez de diversification et trop de greed

The question is not whether light can compute.

The question is: why does it matter now?

The photonics ecosystem is reaching the point where larger and more sophisticated integrated circuits are becoming possible—but greater scale also makes physical errors, drift, loss, and fabrication variation harder to control.

QP2.0 was built to address that challenge.

#quantum #quantumcomputing #photonics

text:
“Putting out to Pasture”  – A cartoon that illustrates how silicon is soon reaching the end of its life for computing.

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure.

That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale.

The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti  and @PsiQuantum  are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.”

Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs  and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving.

The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon.

#FutureOfComputing #AIInfrastructure #iantoons

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure. That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale. The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti and @PsiQuantum are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.” Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving. The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon. #FutureOfComputing #AIInfrastructure #iantoons"}}}, "rest_id": "2073084679486316594", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "55", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The silicon photonics supply chain diagram is one of the most important investment maps in technology right now (Save this).

Every AI data center being built depends on what's in that chart and the companies inside it are just starting to show what the revenue ramp looks like.

Here are the 10 stocks I am watching.

1. Broadcom (AVGO): Broadcom's AI revenue rocketed 106% to $8.4 billion in its most recent quarter, driven by custom accelerator chips for Google, Meta, and Anthropic, plus AI networking silicon. It is one of the few companies doing both the switch silicon and the photonics integration needed for co-packaged optics at hyperscale. The silicon photonics diagram shows Broadcom appearing across multiple layers, PIC design, laser sources, photodiode, and EIC design.

2. MACOM Technology (MTSI): MACOM is quietly positioning itself at the inflection point of the 1.6T and 3.2T optical transceiver transition. In March 2026 it launched 448G PAM4 modulator drivers among the first in the industry and joined Broadcom, Cisco, and Semtech in the 400G Optical MSA standards consortium. In June 2026 it introduced hot via chip scale technology, eliminating wire bonds in its AlGaAs packaging.

3. Marvell Technology (MRVL): Marvell is the DSP engine inside many of the optical modules in that chart. Its custom AI silicon, built for hyperscalers who want to own their accelerator architecture is the fastest growing part of its business. It sits at the EIC design layer and supplies the digital signal processing that makes high-speed optical links work reliably at scale. As the industry shifts from 400G to 800G and 1.6T, every step requires more sophisticated DSPs and Marvell captures more revenue per module shipped.

4. Keysight Technologies (KEYS): Every optical module that ships from every fab in that chart has to be tested before it reaches a data center. Keysight is the global leader in electronic and photonic test equipment. As optical speeds push to 800G and beyond, the testing instrumentation has to keep pace and it commands premium pricing.

5. FormFactor (FORM): FormFactor is the most underappreciated name in this entire chart. Q1 2026 revenue was $226 million, up 32% year over year, beating estimates with non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 versus $0.44 expected. It acquired Keystone Photonics in December 2025, becoming the leading wafer-level silicon photonics test platform for co-packaged optics production. Its partnership with Advantest created the world's fastest automated photonic alignment test system with nine-axis nano-precision. Over 100 of the world's leading silicon photonics manufacturers use FormFactor systems meaning every CPO chip that ships from any fab in that diagram gets tested on FormFactor equipment.

6. Teradyne (TER): Teradyne sits in the E/O Testing layer alongside Keysight and FormFactor. As silicon photonics chips get more complex, integrating lasers, modulators, photodetectors and electronic drivers on a single chip,  the test complexity explodes. Teradyne's automated test equipment platforms are expanding from traditional semiconductor testing into photonic integrated circuit validation. It also has a robotics division, which ties the silicon photonics thesis directly back to the humanoid supply chain story.

7. EXFO (EXFO): EXFO is a fiber optic and network testing specialist that has been building testing platforms specifically for coherent optical and silicon photonics applications. It sits directly in the E/O Testing box of the supply chain map. It's a smaller cap, which means the upside from the optical buildout is amplified relative to its size. It is one of the few companies that tests live network optical performance meaning it gets pulled in not just at the component manufacturing stage but every time a data center expands or upgrades.

8. Foxconn Industrial Internet (FXCOF): Foxconn appears twice in that supply chain diagram in both Photonics Assembly and Optical Interconnect and that is before you even consider its server business. Q1 2026 revenue hit $66.6 billion, up 29.7% year over year, with AI servers now representing more than 50% of total server revenue. Its silicon photonics CPO switches entered mass production in Q3 2026 with full year shipments forecast at 10,000 units. It is boosting capex 30% specifically for AI infrastructure. Almost no retail investors think of Foxconn as a silicon photonics play and that's the opportunity.

9. Sumitomo Electric (SMTOY): Sumitomo appears in two places on that supply chain diagram as an optical interconnect component supplier and in the laser/photodiode layer. The company has been a foundational supplier to the fiber optic industry for decades and is now scaling its silicon photonics packaging and optical connectivity capabilities for AI data center applications.

10. Synopsys (SNPS): Every silicon photonics chip has to be designed before it can be manufactured. Synopsys is the dominant EDA software company for photonic integrated circuit design and sits at the very top left of that supply chain map. The shift toward co-packaged optics means optical and electronic chip design have to be co-simulated, exactly the kind of complex, multi physics workflow Synopsys is built for. Its photonics design tools are already embedded across every major chip company in that diagram. The more complex the chip, the more customers pay for Synopsys tools.

Every AI data center being built depends on what's in that chart and the companies inside it are just starting to show what the revenue ramp looks like. Here are the 10 stocks I am watching. 1. Broadcom (AVGO): Broadcom's AI revenue rocketed 106% to $8.4 billion in its most recent quarter, driven by custom accelerator chips for Google, Meta, and Anthropic, plus AI networking silicon. It is one of the few companies doing both the switch silicon and the photonics integration needed for co-packaged optics at hyperscale. The silicon photonics diagram shows Broadcom appearing across multiple layers, PIC design, laser sources, photodiode, and EIC design. 2. MACOM Technology (MTSI): MACOM is quietly positioning itself at the inflection point of the 1.6T and 3.2T optical transceiver transition. In March 2026 it launched 448G PAM4 modulator drivers among the first in the industry and joined Broadcom, Cisco, and Semtech in the 400G Optical MSA standards consortium. In June 2026 it introduced hot via chip scale technology, eliminating wire bonds in its AlGaAs packaging. 3. Marvell Technology (MRVL): Marvell is the DSP engine inside many of the optical modules in that chart. Its custom AI silicon, built for hyperscalers who want to own their accelerator architecture is the fastest growing part of its business. It sits at the EIC design layer and supplies the digital signal processing that makes high-speed optical links work reliably at scale. As the industry shifts from 400G to 800G and 1.6T, every step requires more sophisticated DSPs and Marvell captures more revenue per module shipped. 4. Keysight Technologies (KEYS): Every optical module that ships from every fab in that chart has to be tested before it reaches a data center. Keysight is the global leader in electronic and photonic test equipment. As optical speeds push to 800G and beyond, the testing instrumentation has to keep pace and it commands premium pricing. 5. FormFactor (FORM): FormFactor is the most underappreciated name in this entire chart. Q1 2026 revenue was $226 million, up 32% year over year, beating estimates with non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 versus $0.44 expected. It acquired Keystone Photonics in December 2025, becoming the leading wafer-level silicon photonics test platform for co-packaged optics production. Its partnership with Advantest created the world's fastest automated photonic alignment test system with nine-axis nano-precision. Over 100 of the world's leading silicon photonics manufacturers use FormFactor systems meaning every CPO chip that ships from any fab in that diagram gets tested on FormFactor equipment. 6. Teradyne (TER): Teradyne sits in the E/O Testing layer alongside Keysight and FormFactor. As silicon photonics chips get more complex, integrating lasers, modulators, photodetectors and electronic drivers on a single chip, the test complexity explodes. Teradyne's automated test equipment platforms are expanding from traditional semiconductor testing into photonic integrated circuit validation. It also has a robotics division, which ties the silicon photonics thesis directly back to the humanoid supply chain story. 7. EXFO (EXFO): EXFO is a fiber optic and network testing specialist that has been building testing platforms specifically for coherent optical and silicon photonics applications. It sits directly in the E/O Testing box of the supply chain map. It's a smaller cap, which means the upside from the optical buildout is amplified relative to its size. It is one of the few companies that tests live network optical performance meaning it gets pulled in not just at the component manufacturing stage but every time a data center expands or upgrades. 8. Foxconn Industrial Internet (FXCOF): Foxconn appears twice in that supply chain diagram in both Photonics Assembly and Optical Interconnect and that is before you even consider its server business. Q1 2026 revenue hit $66.6 billion, up 29.7% year over year, with AI servers now representing more than 50% of total server revenue. Its silicon photonics CPO switches entered mass production in Q3 2026 with full year shipments forecast at 10,000 units. It is boosting capex 30% specifically for AI infrastructure. Almost no retail investors think of Foxconn as a silicon photonics play and that's the opportunity. 9. Sumitomo Electric (SMTOY): Sumitomo appears in two places on that supply chain diagram as an optical interconnect component supplier and in the laser/photodiode layer. The company has been a foundational supplier to the fiber optic industry for decades and is now scaling its silicon photonics packaging and optical connectivity capabilities for AI data center applications. 10. Synopsys (SNPS): Every silicon photonics chip has to be designed before it can be manufactured. Synopsys is the dominant EDA software company for photonic integrated circuit design and sits at the very top left of that supply chain map. The shift toward co-packaged optics means optical and electronic chip design have to be co-simulated, exactly the kind of complex, multi physics workflow Synopsys is built for. Its photonics design tools are already embedded across every major chip company in that diagram. The more complex the chip, the more customers pay for Synopsys tools. Milk Road Pro is tracking every layer of the silicon photonics supply chain, from Broadcom and Marvell to the testing names most investors still don’t know. text:
I wanted to capture my thoughts on where we are re AI in a paragraph. So here's my take on AI at start of July 2026:

AI is not a chip cycle; it is a full-stack intelligence-production, delegation, and embodiment cycle. The monetisable unit of AI is evolving from tokens and chat sessions into workflow outcomes, research throughput, software changes, enterprise decisions, scientific discoveries, factory movements, and robot-hours. Demand begins with consumers, developers, enterprises, governments, labs, hyperscalers, industrial operators, and physical-work customers paying for AI-enabled search, coding, agents, scientific research, drug discovery, productivity, advertising, analytics, model APIs, workflow automation, regulated decision support, cloud consumption, and robotic labor. That demand creates a new software and control layer: long-running inference, multi-turn agents, tool execution, file and web access, code execution, connectors, MCP-style integrations, identity and permissions, secure sandboxes, observability, evals, provenance, audit trails, memory, workflow state, domain data governance, and human-in-the-loop supervision. It then maps into contracted compute capacity and AI factories requiring powered land, interconnection rights, colocation, data-center real estate, utility load growth, firm power, merchant generation, PPAs, onsite generation, nuclear optionality, grid upgrades, substations, transformers, switchgear, cabling, transmission and distribution equipment, EPC, modular construction, inside-the-fence electrical distribution, rack power, power semiconductors, liquid cooling, HVAC, and heat rejection. The semiconductor layer remains essential but is itself a system: accelerators, custom ASICs, CPUs, DPUs, edge silicon, EDA, IP, simulation, foundry capacity, WFE, metrology, process control, advanced packaging, HBM, memory, storage, controllers, probe, test, servers, rack integration, EMS/ODM, and distribution. These systems are connected through scale-up fabrics, retimers, SerDes, timing, memory interfaces, scale-out Ethernet, DCI fabrics, network platforms, optical modules, photonics, lasers, compound semiconductors, cabling, connectors, fiber, passive interconnect, and tower or fiber real estate. The new frontier extends the stack into physical AI: world models, synthetic data, digital twins, robot foundation models, reinforcement-learning environments, teleoperation and demonstration data, simulation platforms, edge inference modules, sensors, cameras, lidar, tactile sensing, actuators, motors, gearboxes, batteries, power electronics, safety systems, certification, fleet orchestration, RaaS, field service, deployment engineering, and robotics manufacturing. Chips remain central, but they are one bottleneck inside a broader conversion system whose scarce assets are the control points that turn data, power, silicon, software tools, simulation, and embodied hardware into reliable, auditable, monetisable work.

That's roughly how I see it at the moment anyway.

text:
Agentic AI is starting to do the work of a full scouting team, now in private markets. @earlysignalsvc  points it at private tech companies and scores them the week they raise, launch, or leave stealth, not the week they trend.

The whole edge in markets is acting while a signal is still fresh, before everyone else has read the same headline. Public equities are already a crowded place to try that, which is the game the DeepSeek Portfolio plays every week. Doing it in venture, at the moment a company raises or leaves stealth, is a much earlier point on the curve.

Forty-four companies ranked this week, each on six scored dimensions plus a potential and a scoop score. Photonics for AI data centers, infrastructure for long-running agents, on-device writing tools. Systematic scoring pushed all the way to the front of the funnel.

Good work. Worth a look.

text:
This post from relatively unknown account @0xBADB01E is going viral within the AI community - here's why

The core claim is genuinely contrarian and flips how we think about scaling architectures and supply chain constraints: 

> premise: the wires between chips matter more than the chips themselves or the memory attached to them

> if you designed an AI inference machine starting from the interconnect and working backwards, you'd build something radically different (and cheaper) than an Nvidia GPU

> GPUs are terrible at generating tokens - when an LLM generates text one token at a time (decode), a Blackwell GPU uses under 20% of its math capability as the compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from memory

IOW, you bought a Ferrari engine and it spends 80% of its time waiting at the gas pump

> the intuitive answer is "add faster memory" (more HBM) but the smarter answer is: since a single chip can't have infinite memory bandwidth, split the model across many chips and let each chip handle a slice

> the problem then shifts because the constraint is no longer memory bandwidth, it's how fast chips can talk to each other (the interconnect)

> Nvidia's interconnect is the wrong approach - NVLink is a firehose with huge bandwidth, but high latency (delay per message)

> that was a deliberate choice for training, but not ideal for inference where the data crossing between chips per token is absurdly tiny 

It's like paying a $50 shipping-and-handling fee on a $1 product

The contrarian take is that everyone says "decode is memory-bandwidth bound," but once you shard the model, per-user speed is gated by how quickly the tiny inter-chip messages complete, not by how fat the memory pipe is - decode is latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound

> if latency is what matters, you can build around slow, cheap memory (LPDDR instead of HBM), because its access latency is about the same as HBM's; only its bandwidth is worse

> then you only need enough FLOPs to match that memory

The above two statements have big implications:

1. this is a direct thesis against HBM's centrality in the compute buildout

2. the packaging supply chain that's currently the binding constraint on Nvidia's output matters much less

3. for photonics, it can potentially go either way - depends on if optical is focused on improving bandwidth (bearish) or latency (bullish)

I don't know this account but this post was interesting and intelligent so I'm here for the conversation that follows

As an example,", "id_str": "2073990357398982797", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "0xBADB01E", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "2073970411138949533", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "1811787044722479105", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 54, "reply_count": 138, "retweet_count": 192, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1811787044722479105"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzM5OTAzNTcxNzI1NTE2ODA=", "text": "First you have to understand that modern LLM inference already disaggregates weights as models outgrew single chips years ago. You shard either by layer (pipeline parallelism) or by slicing every layer (tensor parallelism), and the two do very different things. As an example, let’s look at Llama 3.3. It has 70B of weights and at FP8 that’s 70 GB of memory which is enough to fit on a single H100. Now that H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM, so the fastest it can ever decode for one user is 70/3.35 ≈ 21 ms/token or ~48 tok/s while using under 1% of its FLOPs. Now if we pipeline it across 8 chips: each chip holds ~8.75 GB, which means it only needs 1/8th the bandwidth and 1/8th the FLOPs to sustain the same aggregate throughput. Now crucially the token/sec a user gets is limited by the amount of data that crosses the link. In current LLMs all that is a small amount of activations for LLama 3.3 it’s ~8 KB per token…. Yes, you read that right it’s 8 KILOBYTES we are sending over a <900 GB/s link. That’s only 9 ns of serialization time but the overhead of 224G PAM4 SerDes adds ~100 ns per link traversal with RS-FEC which is 11x longer than the payload itself. And then you have the NVSwitch adding ~300 ns per hop and you need to pay twice. That’s ~600 ns of just hardware latency wrapped around 9 ns of data making a 98% tax before software even shows up. Then NCCL’s collective stack turns 600 ns into 10-20+ us… all to move 8 kilobytes lol. For comparison 8 KB serializes over 10 Gigabit Ethernet NRZ, in just 6.6 us. Pipeline parallelism however doesn’t make a single user faster as the token still needs to visit every layer in the sequence, so per-user speed is still weights / per-chip bandwidth. To get more speed per user token you need to use tensor parallelism and have all the chips work on the same layer simultaneously. TP costs you 2 all reduce OPs per layer, 160 per token on llama 3, that’s still kilobytes of traffic but with NVLink overhead it’s a massive tax and why pipeline parallelism on most models still gives more interactivity per user. However, this gives you a huge latency lever to pull that scales tokens per second with interconnect speed instead of memory BW. The clever amongst you might have also realized that sharding doesn’t just cut memory bandwidth per chip it also cuts FLOPs per chip and is why we have such bad MFU on decode. So once you’ve sized the link for the memory, you need to size the compute for it too. This is called “balancing the pipeline”, and currently no shipping chip does it because they were all designed as standalone monsters. Remember Tokens/sec = ~aggregate memory BW / bytes touched per token. At batch 64 in FP4 you need ~250 FLOPs per byte, and Blackwell ships 1,250. Provisioned 5x more than the narrow pipe of HBM. Nobody saturates shit cause they are all building around HBM. So now it all comes full circle. Parallelism reduces memory bw pressure and thus FLOPs but increases interconnect latency pressure. Despite having HBM and GigaSERDES we aren’t actually doing more work lol. But if you really wanted to balance the pipeline you need to match the memory bandwidth, the flops, and most importantly the interconnect. So what does that look like ? Well if you build around LPDDR’s lower bandwidth, lower your interconnect latency, you actually can beat Nvidia on decode with a fraction of the silicon."}}}, "rest_id": "2073990357398982797", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1484962", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074201112006242337", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5046", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$BURU @NUBURUlasers is extending its laser platform from counter-UAS to underwater autonomy.

Through Lyocon Italian laser-tech factory and photonics center of excellence, NUBURU is working with Suncubes to connect blue-laser technology, beam-control and optical power transmission into new Defense & Security applications.

✅Recent PRs highlight two strategic directions:
- Laser Arm: vehicle-integrated directed-energy and counter-UAS systems.
- DEEP LIGHT: blue-laser power-beaming for underwater autonomy, subsea power and maritime infrastructure.

The opportunity is not only one project. It is the potential validation of NUBURU blue-laser technology in mission-critical environments where power, endurance and optical performance matter.
Lyocon provides the laser engine. 
☀️SunCubes provides beam-control and optical power capabilities. NUBURU builds the platform.

Subject to definitive agreements, technical validation, regulatory clearances and applicable approvals.
#NUBURU #Lyocon #SunCubes #BlueLaser  #DefenseTech #DirectedEnergy  #CounterUAS  #UnderwaterAutonomy

text:
amazing!

"MIT researchers developed 2 new photonic coupling technologies, the Evanescent Coupler and GRIN Coupler, that could enable future chip packages to exceed 1 petabit per second (1,000 Tb/s) of communication bandwidth."

"Instead of sending data through power hungry copper wires, the system uses light (photonics) to move information between chips, dramatically increasing bandwidth while reducing energy consumption and heat."

"Researchers designed the technology to be compatible with existing CMOS semiconductor manufacturing, potentially making ultra-high bandwidth photonic chip packages practical for future AI accelerators, supercomputers and hyperscale data centers."

text:
There's a company almost no one is talking about, trading near NAV, that sells into three of the hottest markets right now: AI chipmaking, defense batteries, and photonics.

And it has several unfair advantages:
→ $100M Department of Energy grant.
→ Samsung SDI building and backing its battery factory.
→ A 2028 law that bans the US military from buying Chinese battery cells.

That company is @ForgeNano, and the vehicle taking it public at a $1.2B valuation is Archimedes Tech SPAC Partners II $ATII, run by the same team that took SoundHound $SOUN public in 2022.

Forge Nano sells atomic-scale coatings, protective layers thousands of times thinner than a human hair, that make batteries last longer and chips fail less.

$ATII trades at $10.88/share today against a trust worth ~$10.63/share, so the downside from here is ~2%. And you can buy $ATIIW if you want the leveraged upside via warrants.

I've done deep dives (and bought) into SPAC setups like this before:
→ Boost Run $BRUN pre-merger (originally $WLAC).
→ Exascale Labs $BCAR, still pre-merger.

My full 6k-word deep dive on $ATII will be released tomorrow. Link in bio to read it when it comes out!

Thanks to @SPAC_zilla and @stepnotonpets for the idea!
